title: Girls Just Want To Have Sums

jokes.text: Dad, taking the family to opening night is the nicest thing you've ever done for us. Or will ever do.
jokes.setup: Lisa compliments Homer on doing something nice for the family
jokes.punchline: Immediately undercuts it by saying it's also the nicest thing he'll ever do
jokes.explanation: Classic Lisa - backhanded compliment that's both sweet and cutting. Perfect character voice with efficient structure.

jokes.text: Hey, it says here the book was written by Tom Stoppard. / This isn't a book, it's a play. / Book.
jokes.setup: Lisa corrects Homer's confusion about book vs. play
jokes.punchline: Homer's stubborn single-word insistence he's right
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer stubbornness - the single word 'Book' encapsulates his character perfectly.

jokes.text: They're coming down the aisles. What if they want to interact with me? Looking through my purse, looking through my purse.... keep walking, monkey. Don't worry, mom, they all passed by.
jokes.setup: Marge panics about interactive theater performers approaching
jokes.punchline: Her awkward avoidance strategy and calling performer 'monkey'
jokes.explanation: Great Marge anxiety moment - the 'keep walking, monkey' is perfectly uncomfortable and character-specific.

jokes.text: ...the circle of... knife!
jokes.setup: Parody of Lion King's 'Circle of Life' builds expectation
jokes.punchline: Subverts with violent 'knife' instead of 'life'
jokes.explanation: Brilliant subversion of Disney's most famous song - perfect economy and dark twist on familiar material.

jokes.text: I love the use of streamers as blood. It robs the violence of its power. / I'm drenched in blood!
jokes.setup: Lisa intellectualizes the artistic choice to minimize violence
jokes.punchline: Homer immediately contradicts her by being covered in fake blood
jokes.explanation: Perfect Lisa-Homer contrast - her highbrow analysis immediately undercut by his visceral experience.

jokes.text: Hey, check it out! I'm scratcher or something!
jokes.setup: Bart gets caught up in the theatrical experience
jokes.punchline: His casual dismissal of not knowing which character he's supposed to be
jokes.explanation: Solid Bart moment - his casual attitude toward getting basic facts wrong is very in character.

jokes.text: Well, I remember getting a 'b' or two in math. / Well, of course you did. You are a girl.
jokes.setup: Successful director modestly mentions getting some B's in math
jokes.punchline: Skinner's casually sexist response
jokes.explanation: Classic Skinner foot-in-mouth moment - his oblivious sexism perfectly sets up the episode conflict.

jokes.text: There, that should put the matter behind us.
jokes.setup: Skinner has just made an incredibly offensive statement
jokes.punchline: His complete obliviousness that he's made things worse
jokes.explanation: Perfect Skinner delusion - his confidence that he's resolved the situation is hilariously misplaced.

jokes.text: I don't know why girls are worse. One thing we can all agree on, though, this little lady surely looks pretty tonight. Am I right?
jokes.setup: Skinner tries to dig himself out of sexist hole
jokes.punchline: Makes it infinitely worse with patronizing compliment
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation - each attempt to fix his sexism makes it exponentially worse. 'Little lady' is perfectly condescending.

jokes.text: Don't worry, I have a plan: Pretend I agree with them. / Well, you'd better hurry. Look what they've done to your car. / No, that's how it always looks. / How sad.
jokes.setup: Chalmers points out vandalism to Skinner's car
jokes.punchline: Revelation that the car always looks terrible, followed by Chalmers' deadpan pity
jokes.explanation: Perfect misdirection - we expect vandalism reveal, get character detail about Skinner's pathetic car. Chalmers' 'How sad' is perfectly delivered pity.

jokes.text: What is the source of this 'illusion,' or as I call it, the biggest lie ever told? / You're a worse version of Hitler!
jokes.setup: Skinner tries to frame gender gap as illusion
jokes.punchline: Immediate Hitler comparison for mild educational opinion
jokes.explanation: Good escalation joke - the immediate jump to Hitler comparison is absurdly extreme for the mild offense.

jokes.text: See?! The principal's a tranny. / Am I wearing women's clothes? I didn't notice.
jokes.setup: Protester makes accusation about Skinner's gender presentation
jokes.punchline: Skinner's complete obliviousness to his own appearance
jokes.explanation: Typical Skinner obliviousness, though the setup relies on dated terminology that hasn't aged well.

jokes.text: It's the differences, of which there are none, that make the sameness exceptional. / Just tell me what to say.
jokes.setup: Skinner attempts to thread impossible logical needle
jokes.punchline: Completely gives up and begs for help
jokes.explanation: Brilliant contradiction - 'differences of which there are none' is perfectly nonsensical. His immediate surrender is very Skinner.

jokes.text: Yes, yes, yes, I said 'diarrhea.'
jokes.setup: Chalmers mentions 'diarrhea of the mouth' over PA
jokes.punchline: Acknowledges the audience reaction to saying the word
jokes.explanation: Solid meta-moment - Chalmers acknowledging the childish reaction to bathroom humor is appropriately self-aware.

jokes.text: This rocks! No more stupid girl classes, like ballroom dancing. / No, that's still mandatory for everybody. / Willie?
jokes.setup: Bart celebrates escaping 'girl classes' like ballroom dancing
jokes.punchline: Willie reveals everyone still has to take it, Bart's confused appeal
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection - Bart's excitement gets immediately deflated. His confused 'Willie?' is perfect.

jokes.text: Now, this is why I got into education.
jokes.setup: Context suggests Willie is referring to the gender segregation chaos
jokes.punchline: Willie's enthusiasm for educational dysfunction
jokes.explanation: Perfect Willie cynicism - his joy at educational chaos is very much in character for the disgruntled groundskeeper.

jokes.text: Now I can walk down the hall with 'Bart junior' hanging out. / Isn't that right, Bart junior?
jokes.setup: Bart celebrates freedom from girls at school
jokes.punchline: Reveals he's talking to/about his exposed genitals
jokes.explanation: Typical juvenile Bart humor - naming his genitals is crude but age-appropriate for the character.

jokes.text: Well, do you know what a man invented? Actual paper. / Which goes great with another male invention: the car.
jokes.setup: Homer counters liquid paper invention with actual paper
jokes.punchline: Continues the pattern by pairing windshield wipers with cars
jokes.explanation: Good escalating pattern - Homer's male chauvinist argument gains momentum through increasingly absurd comparisons.

jokes.text: Why did women invent sleeping on the couch?
jokes.setup: Homer lists male inventions in sexist rant
jokes.punchline: Marge's cutting response about relationship consequences
jokes.explanation: Excellent comeback - perfectly cuts through Homer's nonsense with relationship reality. Peak Marge wit with great economy.

jokes.text: All right, I'm going to open this cage, but no biting! / You're not the boss of my teeth!
jokes.setup: Willie warns boys about biting when releasing them
jokes.punchline: Boy's defiant declaration of dental autonomy
jokes.explanation: Great absurdist line - 'You're not the boss of my teeth' is wonderfully illogical kid logic with perfect delivery.

jokes.text: Cathy Guisewite!
jokes.setup: Girls excited about paintings by female artists like Frida Kahlo and Georgia O'Keeffe
jokes.punchline: Including comic strip creator Cathy among fine artists
jokes.explanation: Smart cultural joke - placing newspaper comic creator among serious artists highlights absurd tokenism.

jokes.text: How do numbers make you feel? What does a plus sign smell like? Is the number seven odd...or just different?
jokes.setup: New age approach to math education
jokes.punchline: Increasingly ridiculous questions culminating in 'seven is just different'
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation - each question gets more absurd, with 'seven is just different' being a perfect punchline that subverts math terminology.

jokes.text: Sounds like you're trying to derail our 'self-esteem engine.'
jokes.setup: Lisa questions the teaching method
jokes.punchline: Melanie's ridiculous 'self-esteem engine' metaphor
jokes.explanation: Good educational buzzword satire - 'self-esteem engine' perfectly captures empty pedagogy speak.

jokes.text: He forgot the volume of the carrot nose, one-third base times height. / Math, I have missed you!
jokes.setup: Lisa disguised as boy corrects math problem
jokes.punchline: Her passionate declaration of love for mathematics
jokes.explanation: Perfect Lisa moment - her joy at actual mathematics after the girls' school nonsense is very character-specific.

jokes.text: I don't have any opinions anymore. All I know is that no one is better than anyone else, And everyone is the best at everything. / Not you. You're the worst! Now get poisonin' those squirrels.
jokes.setup: Skinner's broken attempt at diplomatic neutrality
jokes.punchline: Willie immediately contradicts the sentiment and gives harsh orders
jokes.explanation: Perfect deflation - Skinner's broken diplomatic speak gets immediately undercut by Willie's harsh reality and squirrel poisoning detail.

jokes.text: Come on, baby. The only math you need is you plus me equals forever. / Oh, Homey!
jokes.setup: Young Homer tries to seduce Marge away from studying
jokes.punchline: Cheesy math pickup line that actually works on Marge
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer cheesiness - the math pickup line is corny but perfectly in character, and Marge's response shows why it worked.

jokes.text: Mom, I don't think this will fool anybody. / Hey, who's the rude dude with the attitude?
jokes.setup: Lisa doubts her boy disguise will work
jokes.punchline: Homer immediately doesn't recognize his own daughter
jokes.explanation: Good ironic reversal - Lisa's worry immediately proven wrong by Homer's obliviousness. His 'rude dude' phrasing is perfectly dated.

jokes.text: You never take our kids to the movies. / Let's not fight in front of my cool new friend.
jokes.setup: Marge criticizes Homer's parenting in front of disguised Lisa
jokes.punchline: Homer wants to avoid fighting in front of his own daughter he doesn't recognize
jokes.explanation: Nice layered irony - Homer's attempt at discretion is happening in front of the very child he neglects, but he doesn't know it.

jokes.text: I'm... Jake. / That's your christian name. What's your surname? / Y...man. Jake Boyman.
jokes.setup: Lisa trying to invent a masculine name on the spot
jokes.punchline: Her desperate combination creating the obviously fake 'Jake Boyman'
jokes.explanation: Good nervous improvisation humor. The name is obviously fake but sounds plausible enough that kids would accept it.

jokes.text: You have Toilet paper on your shoe. / yeah, I guess I do. I'm going to call you 'Toilet.' / My name isn't Toilet. It's Jake. / Hey, a talking toilet!
jokes.setup: Jimbo notices toilet paper on new kid's shoe
jokes.punchline: Escalates to calling her Toilet, then 'a talking toilet'
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of schoolyard cruelty - the jump to 'talking toilet' shows how bullies build momentum with nonsense.

jokes.text: A robot with guns for arms shooting a plane made out of guns that fires guns.
jokes.setup: Boy describes what he's drawing
jokes.punchline: Escalating levels of gun violence in the drawing
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist escalation - each element adds more ridiculous violence, perfectly capturing boy drawing stereotypes.

jokes.text: Oh, my god, I was wrong! And by being corrected, I learned! And no one cared about my feelings!
jokes.setup: Lisa gets corrected on math problem and celebrates
jokes.punchline: Her joy at actual learning vs. self-esteem focused education
jokes.explanation: Brilliant educational satire - Lisa's excitement about being wrong and learning perfectly skewers self-esteem-over-education philosophy. Peak character integration.

jokes.text: Lisa? Yeah, we totally had a thing, but I had to break it off. / What the hell are you talking about?! / She got too clingy. Milhouse doesn't do clingy.
jokes.setup: Milhouse claims he had a relationship with Lisa
jokes.punchline: His completely delusional version of their non-relationship
jokes.explanation: Perfect Milhouse delusion - his fantasy relationship revision is hilariously pathetic and completely in character.

jokes.text: Nelson, you're not really angry at me. You're full of rage 'cause your father abandoned you. And because you're poor, so you don't feel like you're good enough. You just want somebody to say 'I love you.' / I love you. / I love you, too, Nelson.
jokes.setup: Lisa psychoanalyzes Nelson to avoid fight
jokes.punchline: Her armchair psychology actually works perfectly
jokes.explanation: Brilliant subversion - Lisa's desperate psychology actually works, creating an unexpectedly sweet moment. Great character insight.

jokes.text: Hey, every joke has a kernel of truth!
jokes.setup: Boys realize Lisa's analysis worked on everyone
jokes.punchline: Jimbo's meta-commentary on psychological insights
jokes.explanation: Solid meta-moment - acknowledges the truth behind the comedy while being appropriately simple for the character.

jokes.text: Hey, no one with a choice should ever have to be a girl. / You'd do that for me? That is so sweet. / You're a boy, nothing is sweet. / That hurt. / Sweet.
jokes.setup: Bart offers to teach Lisa to be a boy
jokes.punchline: Immediate correction of 'sweet' leading to 'That hurt' / 'Sweet' exchange
jokes.explanation: Good brother-sister dynamic - the quick correction and counter-correction shows their relationship perfectly.

jokes.text: I am looking, and don't start a puke war that you cannot finish.
jokes.setup: Bart suggests throwing up in someone's bag
jokes.punchline: Dolph's serious warning about puke war escalation
jokes.explanation: Great threat escalation - treating puke war as serious strategic concern is perfectly absurd schoolboy logic.

jokes.text: Sometimes when I'm with you, I feel like I'm doing it with a dude.
jokes.setup: Homer tries to compliment Marge's intelligence
jokes.punchline: Horrible sexual comparison that's meant as compliment
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer inappropriate compliment - his attempt to praise Marge's mind comes out horribly wrong but very in character.

jokes.text: At least everyone knows I'm smarter than you. / Ho, how did this happen?
jokes.setup: Homer talking to the dog about intelligence
jokes.punchline: Homer's confusion about how the dog got smarter than him
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist moment - Homer's genuine confusion about being outsmarted by the family dog is perfectly stupid.

jokes.text: At least everyone knows I'm smarter than you. / Ho, how did this happen?
jokes.setup: Marge asserting her intellectual superiority over Homer
jokes.punchline: Homer's confused reaction to being outsmarted by his wife
jokes.explanation: Good marital dynamic joke - Homer genuinely confused about when Marge became smarter. His befuddlement is perfectly in character.

jokes.text: Now I've been every ball on the playground!
jokes.setup: Ralph gets hit by various balls during playground fight
jokes.punchline: His cheerful announcement of being hit by all ball types
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ralph logic - finding joy in being repeatedly hit is exactly his character. Great economy and absurdist thinking.

jokes.text: Even though you're only eight, your possibilities are infinite. / 27!
jokes.setup: Lisa gives herself mathematical encouragement
jokes.punchline: Random number exclamation as motivation
jokes.explanation: Good Lisa mathematical enthusiasm - shouting random numbers for encouragement is appropriately nerdy and strange.

jokes.text: Give me your lunch money. / Okay! / I guess I'm gonna have to get this party started. / Forgive me for this. / Okay!
jokes.setup: Lisa tries to bully Ralph who immediately complies
jokes.punchline: She has to escalate because he's too cooperative, he cheerfully agrees to violence
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character interaction - Ralph's eager compliance makes Lisa's bullying attempt impossible, forcing escalation. Very true to both characters.

jokes.text: Thank you. Or should I say thank you!
jokes.setup: Lisa wins math award as Jake
jokes.punchline: Her attempt at masculine speech inflection
jokes.explanation: Mild gender performance joke - Lisa's attempt to sound more masculine is subtle but appropriate character moment.

jokes.text: We've been Yentl'd!
jokes.setup: Discovery that best math student is actually a girl in disguise
jokes.punchline: Reference to Barbra Streisand film about woman disguising as man
jokes.explanation: Good cultural reference - 'Yentl'd' is efficient shorthand for the situation, though requires cultural knowledge.

jokes.text: Toilet is Lisa?! / We've been Yentl'd!
jokes.setup: Students discovering Lisa's true identity
jokes.punchline: Cultural reference to the cross-dressing musical
jokes.explanation: Good cultural reference - 'Yentl'd' is a perfect verb for being deceived by cross-dressing. Shows kids' surprising cultural knowledge.

jokes.text: The only reason Lisa won is because she learned to think like a boy! I turned her into a burping, farting, bullying, math machine!
jokes.setup: Skinner tries to explain Lisa's success
jokes.punchline: Lists increasingly crude behaviors ending with 'math machine'
jokes.explanation: Good escalating list - building from bodily functions to 'math machine' creates nice rhythm and absurd juxtaposition.

jokes.text: I got hit by boy Lisa and girl Lisa.
jokes.setup: Ralph explains his experience with Lisa's identity crisis
jokes.punchline: Matter-of-fact delivery about being hit by both versions
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ralph callback - his simple acceptance of complex situation is very in character with great economy.

jokes.text: Chair fight!
jokes.setup: Lisa giving speech about boys' cruelty
jokes.punchline: Boys immediately proving her point with violence
jokes.explanation: Perfect timing - Lisa's commentary on boys' violence immediately proven by boys starting a chair fight. Great structural irony.

jokes.text: Now enjoy your stupid flautist.
jokes.setup: Lisa finishes her speech about gender and education
jokes.punchline: Dismissive comment about next award category
jokes.explanation: Good ending beat - Lisa's casual dismissal of the flautist award shows her priorities and provides nice deflation after serious speech.

tmdb_overview: The family attends "Stab-A-Lot: The Itchy and Scratchy Musical." The musical's director is a graduate of Springfield Elementary and Principal Skinner puts his foot in mouth when he makes sexist comments about her education and that of women in general. He tries to make the situation better, but only buries himself deeper and deeper until he is fired and replaced by Women's Educational Expert Melanie Upfoot. The first thing she does is split the school into girls and the boys. Lisa dislikes the way girls are being taught math and she ventures over to the boy's side of the school in search of a challenge. Disguising herself as Jake Boyman she starts attending the boy's school but while she finds the math problems more interesting, it is being a boy that is a greater challenge.
